---
title: "Measuring Community Services Across Regions"
subtitle: "Evidence from a Multi-Classification Approach"
by-author:
  - name:
      literal: "Jane Doe"
  - name:
      literal: "John Smith"
orcid: "0000-0000-0000-0000"
affiliation: "Department of Example Studies  •  Example Institute  •  Example University"
venue: "Example Conference 2026"
poster-date: "June 2026"
hero-line: |
  Classification choice shapes measured variation. Under two thirds of
  specifications, the core finding holds; under the rest, it reverses.
  **Measurement decides which story you see.**
footer-contact: "jane.doe@example.edu  ·  orcid.org/0000-0000-0000-0000"
footer-affiliation: "Example Institute  ·  Example University"
footer-resources: "R package: `examplepkg`  ·  github.com/example/examplepkg"
# logo-left: "figures/logo-left.png"
# logo-right: "figures/logo-right.png"
format:
  poster-typst:
    keep-typ: true
---

```{=typst}
#grid(
  columns: (1fr, 1fr, 1fr, 1fr),
  gutter: 12mm,

  // ============================================================
  // COLUMN 1 — Motivation, Question
  // ============================================================
  [
    #section-title[Motivation]

    Researchers almost always pick one classification and report
    results. But classifications disagree — and when they do, the
    substantive conclusion can follow the label rather than the data.

    #v(4mm)

    #callout[
      #text(size: 26pt)[
        *The puzzle.* Classifications disagree on
        #text(weight: "bold", fill: palette.accent)[12.6%] of units.
        Does that disagreement change what we conclude?
      ]
    ]

    #v(6mm)
    #section-title[Question]

    Using two outcomes that differ sharply in robustness, what does
    between-group variation actually look like, and which outcomes
    need a continuous measure to describe honestly?
  ],

  // ============================================================
  // COLUMN 2 — Design, Dimensions
  // ============================================================
  [
    #section-title[Design]

    We assemble administrative data from two recent fiscal years and
    compare three classification schemes side-by-side. Our analysis
    uses specification-curve methods to show how conclusions vary
    across measurement choices.

    #v(4mm)
    #section-title[Dimensions]

    #dim-chip("1", "Staffing", "Permanent staff per 10,000 eligible residents.")
    #dim-chip("2", "Contiguity", "Share of service sites within 10 miles of the central office.")
    #dim-chip("3", "Distance", "Average distance from site to central office, in miles.")
  ],

  // ============================================================
  // COLUMN 3 — Key findings
  // ============================================================
  [
    #section-title[Key Finding 1]

    The variation holds in #text(weight: "bold")[67%] of specifications
    — and reverses in a non-trivial share of the remaining cases.

    #v(6mm)

    #grid(
      columns: (1fr, 1fr),
      gutter: 6mm,
      stat-card("67%", "specifications preserve the finding"),
      stat-card("12.6%", "units where schemes disagree"),
    )

    #v(8mm)

    #section-title[Key Finding 2]

    Outcomes that look stable under one classification become noisy
    under another. Continuous measures recover information that
    categorical labels throw away.
  ],

  // ============================================================
  // COLUMN 4 — Implications, Resources
  // ============================================================
  [
    #section-title[Implications]

    - Choice of classification is a research-design decision, not a
      neutral accounting step.
    - Sensitivity analyses across classifications should be routine.
    - Continuous measures should be preferred where the underlying
      construct is continuous.

    #v(6mm)

    #callout(
      color: palette.primary,
      fill: rgb("#eaf0f5"),
    )[
      #text(size: 26pt)[
        *Bottom line.* Report your classification choice, report a
        sensitivity analysis, and prefer continuous measures where
        they exist.
      ]
    ]

    #v(6mm)

    #section-title[Resources]

    - Code and data: `github.com/example/examplepkg`
    - Replication archive: Harvard Dataverse (DOI on request)
    - Preprint: SSRN (link on request)
  ],
)
```
